title: 19_post_empty_body.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: The Server Logs Show `req.body = {}` But We Sent Data

The sign-up form POSTs `{ email, password }` to `/api/users`. The form validates client-side, the submit fires, the network tab confirms the request reached the server with a 400 response. The backend logs say `req.body = {}` — no email, no password — and rejects the request with `"email is required"`.

You wrote `fetch(url, { method: "POST", body: form })` where `form` is a JS object. Two things are missing: (1) the body has to be a **string** (or `FormData`, `Blob`, etc.) — JS object literals are not valid HTTP bodies; (2) the **`Content-Type` header** has to tell the server how to interpret the body (`application/json`). Without these, the server's JSON body parser receives nothing it can parse — the body is silently empty. Add the two missing pieces.
traceback:
Browser: POST /api/users Request body sent: "[object Object]" ← fetch coerced the object via String() No Content-Type header sent. Backend (express + body-parser): POST /api/users 400 18ms req.headers['content-type'] = undefined req.body = {} ← body-parser skipped — no Content-Type match Rejected: { error: "email is required" } UI shows:

email is required

User is baffled — they typed the email; the network tab shows the request fired.
correctOutput:
Browser: POST /api/users Request body sent: '{"email":"ada@example.com","password":"..."}' Content-Type: application/json Backend (express + body-parser): POST /api/users 201 24ms req.headers['content-type'] = "application/json" req.body = { email: "ada@example.com", password: "..." } Created user 5042 UI shows:

Welcome!

options.label: JSON.stringify the body AND add `Content-Type: application/json` header
options.resultLog:
Request sent:
  POST /api/users
  Content-Type: application/json
  body: {"email":"ada@example.com","password":"..."}

Server parses body successfully, creates the user, returns 201.
UI shows 'Welcome!'

Network tab shows the actual JSON body, not `[object Object]`.
options.rationale:
Two changes, both required:
(1) `JSON.stringify({...})` converts the JS object to a **string** — the only thing HTTP can transmit as a body. Without it, `fetch` coerces the object to a string via `String(value)`, which produces the literal text `"[object Object]"` — useless to any JSON parser.
(2) `Content-Type: application/json` tells the server *how* to interpret that string. Most backend body parsers (Express, Fastify, Next.js handlers, Nest) skip parsing entirely when the Content-Type doesn't match a registered parser — they don't guess. Without the header, the body sits unparsed and `req.body` is whatever the parser's default is (often `{}` or `undefined`).
Both are required. Add only the stringify and the body is `"[object Object]"`; add only the header and there's nothing to parse. **JSON.stringify + Content-Type is the standard pair for JSON POSTs.**

options.label: Just `JSON.stringify` the body — the server should auto-detect JSON
options.resultLog:
Request sent:
  POST /api/users
  Content-Type: (none)  — fetch sets `text/plain;charset=UTF-8` as default for string body
  body: {"email":"ada@example.com","password":"..."}

Backend (express + body-parser):
  Content-Type is text/plain → body-parser.json() skips this request.
  req.body = {} or undefined depending on the parser.

UI still shows: 'email is required'.
The data is on the wire; the server just won't parse it.
options.rationale: When you pass a string to `fetch` without a header, the spec defaults `Content-Type: text/plain;charset=UTF-8`. The body now contains valid JSON, but **the server's JSON parser doesn't run on text/plain requests**. The string is sitting on the wire, but `req.body` is empty because no parser claimed the request. **Body parsers route on Content-Type**; you have to set it explicitly. This is one of the most reliably-confusing bugs for juniors because the network tab clearly shows the JSON body — so why does the backend say it's empty? The answer is in the headers.

options.label: Use FormData instead of JSON — let the browser set the headers automatically
options.resultLog:
Request sent:
  POST /api/users
  Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW
  body: ------WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW\r\nContent-Disposition: form-data; name="email"\r\n\r\nada@example.com\r\n----...

Backend (express + body-parser):
  Content-Type is multipart/form-data → body-parser.json() skips.
  Would need multer or formidable middleware to parse multipart.

UI still shows: 'email is required'.
options.rationale: `FormData` IS a valid request body — the browser sets `Content-Type: multipart/form-data` with a boundary automatically — but **multipart/form-data needs its own parser** (multer, formidable, busboy in Node.js). It's not handled by the JSON body parser. Switching the request format also changes the backend handler's expectations: the same endpoint that expects `req.body.email` (JSON) now needs to process a multipart stream. `FormData` is the right choice when you're uploading **files** alongside text fields; for plain JSON payloads, it's the wrong abstraction. Match the body format to what the endpoint documents — JSON in, JSON out for most modern APIs.

hints: Open the network tab. Look at the request body — what does it show? Then look at the request headers — is `Content-Type` set?
Two things missing: `headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }` and `body: JSON.stringify({ email, password })`.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
HTTP bodies are **bytes on the wire** — they have to be encoded as a string (or binary). A JavaScript object literal is a runtime construct; it can't be transmitted as-is. When `fetch` is handed an object as the `body`, it falls back to `String(value)`, which for a plain object produces the literal text `"[object Object]"`. That string is sent as the body. The server's body parser tries to make sense of it (or skips entirely) and either way the data is lost.

The second half of the bug is the missing `Content-Type` header. The HTTP spec doesn't require a body parser to guess what format the body is in; it requires the sender to declare it. Most server frameworks register body parsers by Content-Type — `application/json` routes to a JSON parser, `multipart/form-data` to a multipart parser, `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` to a URL-encoded parser. **No matching parser, no parsed body.** The default state of `req.body` when no parser claims the request is `{}` or `undefined`, depending on the framework.

With no JSON-shaped string AND no JSON Content-Type, the bug is double: the body isn't valid JSON, and even if it were, the server wouldn't try to parse it.

### Why the fix is correct
The two changes together establish the contract:

1. `JSON.stringify({ email, password })` produces `'{"email":"ada@example.com","password":"..."}'` — a valid JSON string the server's parser can consume.
2. `headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }` tells the server to route the body to the JSON parser.

Both are required because they answer different questions:
- **What is in the body?** → the stringified content.
- **How should the server interpret the body?** → the Content-Type header.

Sending only the stringified content gives the server JSON on the wire but no instruction to parse it. Sending only the header tells the server to parse JSON but gives it `"[object Object]"`, which fails to parse and reaches the handler as `{}` or raises a parse error depending on the framework's strictness.

### The full set of valid `body` values for fetch
The `body` option accepts:
- **`string`** — sent as-is. Set Content-Type to tell the server what kind of string it is.
- **`Blob` / `File`** — binary. Content-Type comes from the Blob's `type` property.
- **`FormData`** — multipart/form-data. Browser sets Content-Type and boundary automatically. Use for file uploads.
- **`URLSearchParams`** — application/x-www-form-urlencoded. Browser sets Content-Type. Use for `<form>`-style submissions.
- **`ArrayBuffer`** — binary. Set Content-Type for whatever you're sending.
- **`ReadableStream`** — streaming bodies (advanced; for large uploads).

A plain JavaScript object **is not** in this list. The spec doesn't auto-serialise objects to JSON; you have to do that step.

### What the network tab shows for each kind
- **Object body (the bug)**: Request body shows `"[object Object]"`. Headers show no `Content-Type`. Server logs show `req.body = {}`.
- **Stringified body, no header**: Request body shows the JSON string. Headers show `Content-Type: text/plain;charset=UTF-8` (fetch's default for string bodies). Server logs show `req.body = {}` because the JSON parser doesn't match.
- **Stringified + JSON header** (the fix): Request body shows the JSON string. Headers show `Content-Type: application/json`. Server logs show `req.body = { email: ..., password: ... }`.
- **FormData**: Request body shows multipart-encoded text. Headers show `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=...`. Server needs a multipart parser.

Reading the network tab is the fastest way to diagnose all of these. The request body and the headers tell you exactly what the server is receiving.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and you named both required pieces — stringify and Content-Type. The JSON.stringify-without-header distractor is the most common partial fix; the data shows up in the network tab and the developer assumes the backend is broken because 'I can see the JSON right there.' The FormData distractor is a real choice for file uploads but is the wrong abstraction for plain JSON — and switching to it would force a backend handler change that wouldn't otherwise be needed. One follow-up worth knowing: when you control both the frontend and the backend, **a typed API client** (generated from OpenAPI or built on top of a library like `tRPC`) makes the body/header pairing impossible to get wrong because the client wraps it for you. That's the long-term answer for non-trivial codebases. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## How POST Bodies Work in HTTP

A POST request carries data in its **body**. The body is just bytes on the wire — the HTTP protocol is text-based but agnostic about what's inside the body. The server has to know **two things** to make sense of it:
1. **What's in the body** — the actual bytes (a JSON string, a multipart-encoded form, a binary file, etc.)
2. **How to interpret the body** — communicated via the `Content-Type` header.

Most backend frameworks register body parsers by Content-Type. Express has `body-parser.json()` for `application/json`, `body-parser.urlencoded()` for form-encoded data, and middleware like `multer` for multipart uploads. **No matching parser, no parsed body.**

### What `body` accepts in fetch
The Fetch API's `body` option accepts these types:
- **`string`** — the most common case. You stringify your data and set Content-Type yourself.
- **`Blob` / `File`** — binary or file uploads. Content-Type comes from the Blob's `type`.
- **`FormData`** — multipart/form-data. The browser sets Content-Type and boundary automatically.
- **`URLSearchParams`** — application/x-www-form-urlencoded. Browser sets Content-Type.
- **`ArrayBuffer`** / **`ReadableStream`** — for binary data and streaming uploads.

A plain JavaScript object **is not** a valid body. If you pass one, `fetch` falls back to `String(value)`, producing `"[object Object]"` — useless.

### The canonical JSON POST
```js
const res = await fetch("/api/users", {
  method: "POST",
  headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
  body: JSON.stringify({ email, password }),
});
```
Three pieces: the method, the header, and the stringified body. Memorise this; it's the most-typed-by-hand pattern in any React codebase.

### What happens when you skip pieces

**Skip JSON.stringify**: body becomes `"[object Object]"`. Server can't parse it. `req.body = {}` or a parse error.

**Skip Content-Type**: body is sent as `text/plain` (fetch's default for string bodies). Server's JSON parser skips it (no matching Content-Type). `req.body = {}`.

**Skip both** (the bug): `"[object Object]"` with `text/plain`. Nothing the server can use.

### Diagnosing in the network tab
Open DevTools → Network tab → click the request → look at:
- **Headers**: is `Content-Type` set, and is it what the server expects?
- **Request payload**: is it the JSON string you expected, or `[object Object]`, or something else?

This is faster than logging on the server. If the request payload looks wrong before it even leaves the browser, the problem is client-side. If it looks right but the server still rejects, the problem is the Content-Type or a server-side parser configuration.

### When to use other body formats
- **FormData** — file uploads, or when you want the form-encoded shape `email=ada@example.com&password=...`. For pure JSON, JSON.stringify is shorter and more common.
- **URLSearchParams** — when posting to an endpoint that expects classic HTML-form-encoded data (some legacy APIs, some auth endpoints).
- **Blob** — uploading a single file without the multipart wrapper (e.g. avatar uploads to S3).
- **Plain string with custom Content-Type** — for non-JSON payloads (`text/csv`, `application/xml`, etc.).

### Server-side body parsers
Most frameworks need a body parser middleware registered to make `req.body` populated. In Express:

```js
import express from "express";
const app = express();
app.use(express.json());         // parses application/json
app.use(express.urlencoded());   // parses application/x-www-form-urlencoded
```

Next.js, Nest, Fastify, etc. have similar setup steps. If `req.body` is consistently `{}` or `undefined` on the server, the most likely cause is a missing or misconfigured parser middleware.

### A typed client to make this impossible
For non-trivial codebases, a typed API client (generated from OpenAPI, hand-written, or via `tRPC`) wraps the body/header pairing so individual call sites can't get it wrong:

```ts
// Wrapped once.
async function apiPost<T>(url: string, body: unknown): Promise<T> {
  const res = await fetch(url, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify(body),
  });
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);
  return res.json();
}

// Used everywhere.
const user = await apiPost<User>("/api/users", { email, password });
```

Now new endpoints can't accidentally omit the header or skip the stringify.

### Common variations of this bug
- Object body, no header — `"[object Object]"` on the wire.
- Stringified body, no header — JSON on the wire but the server skips parsing.
- Stringified body, wrong Content-Type (e.g. `text/plain`) — same as above.
- FormData when the endpoint expects JSON — the multipart parser isn't registered.
- Missing body-parser middleware on the server — even a perfect request lands as `req.body = undefined`.
- Form `<input>` values that are numbers stringified as strings (`"42"` not `42`) — a separate type-coercion bug.
- Forgetting `e.preventDefault()` in the form submit handler — the form does a full-page navigation instead of the fetch.

### You just learned
A POST body has to be a string (or another valid body type), and the server has to be told what kind of string via the `Content-Type` header. Both pieces are required: `body: JSON.stringify(payload)` AND `headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }`. The network tab shows you exactly what's on the wire — when in doubt, look there first. For long-term codebases, wrap the pattern in a typed client so individual call sites can't get it wrong.
